skill.name: upgrade-stripe
skill.description: Guide for upgrading Stripe API versions and SDKs
skill.summary: Step-by-step guidance for upgrading Stripe API versions, server SDKs, Stripe.js, and mobile SDKs with minimal integration disruption.
skill.author: stripe

security_audit.summary: Documentation-only skill sourced from Stripe's official ai repository. The 27 'external_commands' findings are markdown inline-code backticks (e.g., `Stripe.api_version`), not shell execution. The 8 'network' findings are links to docs.stripe.com. The HIGH 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags and LOW 'system reconnaissance' flag are false positives triggered by benign English words in headings and lists. No executable code, no secrets, and no malicious intent detected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown backticks misidentified as Ruby/shell backtick execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 27 inline-code backticks in SKILL.md as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. All instances are markdown formatting (e.g., `2026-05-27.dahlia`, `Stripe.api_version`, `stripe.api_version`). The file contains no Ruby, no shell scripts, and no runtime execution. Patterns are documentation examples only.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URL flags are Stripe official documentation links
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 8 hardcoded URLs. All point to docs.stripe.com (Stripe's own documentation domain). The skill is a reference guide; referencing vendor docs is expected behavior, not a security concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: False positive weak-cryptography flag on benign upgrade text
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged 3 locations (lines 3, 153, 153) for 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. SKILL.md contains no cryptographic code. Line 3 is the frontmatter description; lines 153 are upgrade checklist items containing the word 'Update' and 'upgrade' in package management context. No md5, sha1, des, rc4, or any algorithm references exist in the file.

dangerous_patterns.title: False positive system-reconnaissance flag on section heading
dangerous_patterns.description: Static scanner flagged SKILL.md:129 as 'system reconnaissance'. Line 129 is the markdown section heading '### iOS and Android SDKs' discussing mobile SDK versioning. No system queries, enumeration, or reconnaissance patterns exist.

content.user_title: Upgrade Stripe APIs and SDKs Safely
content.value_statement: Upgrading Stripe API versions often breaks integrations. This skill provides version-aware guidance to migrate server SDKs, Stripe.js, and mobile SDKs without downtime.
actual_capabilities: Identify current and target Stripe API versions using date-based naming (e.g., 2026-05-27.dahlia)
Guide server SDK upgrades for Python, Ruby, Node.js, PHP, Java, Go, and .NET
Configure explicit apiVersion parameters in client initialization code
Update Stripe.js to specific major releases (Acacia, Basil, Clover, Dahlia) via script tag or npm
Update iOS, Android, and React Native mobile SDKs following semver conventions
Test new API versions using the Stripe-Version header before changing production defaults
limitations: Documentation-only; does not execute code, run migrations, or modify your codebase
Cannot access your Stripe account, retrieve live API keys, or call the Stripe API
Does not cover payment processors other than Stripe
Does not handle webhook endpoint reconfiguration or secret rotation

use_cases.title: Migrate server SDK from acacia to dahlia
use_cases.description: A backend developer needs to upgrade their Node.js or Python Stripe SDK to the 2026-05-27.dahlia API version without breaking production charges.

use_cases.title: Update Stripe.js script tag for a checkout flow
use_cases.description: A frontend engineer wants to switch their checkout page from the v3 evergreen build to the dahlia release to match their backend API version.

use_cases.title: Bump iOS and Android SDK versions in a mobile app
use_cases.description: A mobile developer needs to update their iOS and Android Stripe SDKs to the latest semver release and understand the React Native 0.x.y breaking-change model.

prompt_templates.title: Beginner version upgrade
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me upgrade my Stripe integration to the latest API version (2026-05-27.dahlia). My current code uses the default account API version.
prompt_templates.scenario: New to Stripe versioning and needs a guided walkthrough

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate Node.js SDK migration
prompt_templates.prompt: I run a Node.js service using the stripe npm package. Walk me through upgrading from API version 2024-12-18.acacia to 2026-05-27.dahlia with minimal disruption, including how to set the apiVersion parameter and test with the Stripe-Version header.
prompt_templates.scenario: Mid-level developer performing a planned upgrade

prompt_templates.title: Advanced staged rollout with header testing
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a staged migration of our multi-tenant billing system from acacia to dahlia. Include a checklist for testing the Stripe-Version header, updating webhook handlers for new event types, and rolling the apiVersion parameter per service.
prompt_templates.scenario: Senior engineer orchestrating a multi-service rollout

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Stripe.js and webhook alignment
prompt_templates.prompt: Our frontend loads Stripe.js v3 and our backend uses acacia. Outline the steps to migrate both to dahlia in lockstep, including which webhook event structures changed and how to keep them backward compatible during the transition.
prompt_templates.scenario: Full-stack engineer aligning frontend and backend versions

output_examples.input: What is the latest Stripe.js major version and which API version does it pair with?
output: Release cadence: biannual major releases (Acacia, Basil, Clover, Dahlia)
You cannot override the Stripe.js-to-API version pairing

output_examples.input: How do I test a new API version in production without changing my default?
output: Use the Stripe-Version request header on individual API calls
Your account default remains unchanged during testing
Switch the apiVersion parameter in code only after validation succeeds

best_practices: Always set the apiVersion parameter explicitly in client initialization code rather than relying on the account default
Test new API versions using the Stripe-Version header before changing the apiVersion in production code
Update webhook handlers to handle new event structures before flipping the API version for live traffic
anti_patterns: Setting a different API version than the SDK release date for strongly-typed languages (Java, Go, .NET) because response objects may not match the SDK types
Skipping the API changelog review when jumping multiple Stripe API versions at once
Hardcoding real test or live secret keys (sk_test_xxx, sk_live_xxx) in client-side or committed code

faq.question: Can I override the API version pairing for Stripe.js?
faq.answer: No. Each Stripe.js major version automatically pairs with its corresponding API version and this association cannot be overridden.

faq.question: How do I test a new API version without changing my account default?
faq.answer: Send the Stripe-Version header with the target version on individual API requests. The header overrides the version for that request only.

faq.question: Do mobile SDKs work with any backend API version?
faq.answer: Yes. All iOS, Android, and React Native Stripe SDKs work with any backend API version unless the documentation explicitly states otherwise.

faq.question: Should I set apiVersion in code or rely on the account default?
faq.answer: Always set the apiVersion parameter explicitly in code. This prevents silent behavior changes when the account default is updated.

faq.question: How often does Stripe release new API versions?
faq.answer: Stripe.js major releases happen biannually (Acacia, Basil, Clover, Dahlia). Server-side API versions follow a date-based schedule with multiple versions coexisting.

faq.question: What happens to webhooks when I change the API version?
faq.answer: Webhook payloads reflect the API version in effect when the event was created. Update your webhook handlers to handle any new event structures before switching the version in production.
